#### 4. @maheshbandari3580 — 14 likes — **reply to** `root`

Hi, I am Mahesh. I am from Hyderabad. I am one of the subscriber to think school I saw this podcast. I am also working in health insurance sector. In this podcast, you missed about proportional deductions. In case a customer is occupied in a higher room category in a hospital, the insurance company will apply proportionate deductions.
At the same time, in this podcast, you discussed cashless process. You mentioned at the time of admission, only the policy copy is enough. But in the cashless process, ID proof, doctor’s prescription, and related reports are compulsory in planned admissions. In emergency admissions, doctor’s prescription and ID proof is compulsory.

One more thing mentioned in this podcast customer does not need to pay anything, but most insurance companies do not pay NME charges. Based on the insurance policy, the surgery cost and room rent charges will change. Based on the MOU, they will decide the package.

These things are missed in the podcast. 

Thank you 
Big fan from Hyderabad

#### 5. @tusharshinde5682 — 6 likes — **reply to** `root`

@ThinkSchool_Hindi ....I think there are some key points you forgot to mention  due to time limitations i.e, 

1. Maternity Coverage for most of the insurers are very lowest
2. Existing Illness and specific illness coverage, permanent exclusions. These terms should have to be included in this podcast.
3. Restoration of cover needs to be explained in detail
4. You must need to highlight one of the utmost important things i.e., Always Read and understand the Policy Wordings thoroughly before purchasing any insurance Policy.
5. Importance Of OPD coverage needs to be explained.
6. Necessary Add-on riders should have to be discussed.

#### 1. @alappuzha9 — 34 likes — **reply to** `root`

I am sure this will be most valuable video, insurance companies are the biggest cheaters in modern India ... #### 2. @shankarnath — 18 likes — **reply to** `UgzeoElNWsrBNV7KFBd4AaABAg`

Hmm .. if one puts oneself in the insurer's place; i.e. offer a 98% delivery of services and if you're called a cheat -- it won't be fair! At a claim settlement ratio of 98%+ (by count of policies) and 90%+ (by amount), term insurers do their bit to ensure the promised amount goes to the beneficiary. The gap/dispute (10%) are often edge cases -- some of them mild (like documentation), some of them hard (pre-existing illness) and rest of them outright concrete (fraud*). Sometimes the insurer is at fault and sometimes the policyholder.

As consumers, we have a few recourses and this podcast's real message is to understand these and plan better. Recapping it here --

1. Have a better understanding of the term insurance policy you are enrolling into (incl. critical illness, suicide clause)
2. Be 100% honest in your application about your condition & disclosures
3. Buy via a broker/agent as it offers a second layer of dispute protection
4. There is a complaint structure in place with ombudsperson & courts. And Shrehith's suggestion is to definitely use it if & when the need arises

- I want to recite an incident that I experienced in my Insurance days on how consumers skim insurance companies. So in Gujarat (I think or maybe Madhya Pradesh), a scheme was launched where every bag of Urea the farmer purchases has an in-built life cover of 50,000 rupees. Let's say a farmer buys 10 bags of Urea in a season and if he/she were to die, the beneficiary can get those Urea bags to the nearby insurance offices and claim the life insurance cover. So the insurance model was created assuming an average of 5,00,000 rupees of life insurance cover. The first year was fine (not too many claims), so was the 2nd year, but here's what happened in year 3.

In the 3rd year, someone figured that the system can be gamed. So when a farmer next died in that village, all the villagers would get together with whatever number of Urea bags they had with them and would give it all to the beneficiary. This would often come to 300 - 400 bags of Urea. The beneficiary would then approach the insurer and claim 1.5 to 2 crores. This caught on and the insurer started receiving claims of 300, 400, even 800 bags on one occasion making the entire exercise unviable for the insurer. Ergo, the insurer took the losses, closed down the program and the farmers lost their life insurance protection because some of them tried to cheat their way in

My point is -- disputes happen both ways. Some initiated by the insurer, some initiated by the consumer. As consumers, we should hold our end of the deal (point 1 & 2 above) and use the help of authorities (point 3 & 4 above) as and when needed for a resolution.

Sorry! I've seen all sides of it, having been a 1. consumer (multiple term & health insurance policies), 2. distributor (ICICI Bank, GE Consumer Finance & ET Money) and 3. insurer (Chubb, Bharti AXA & HDFC Life) -- hence the longish message

Thank you for the appreciation, I'm glad you liked this podcast! 🙌

#### 3. @shankarnath — 17 likes — **reply to** `UgzTQerhOUFVPfGxdh54AaABAg`

Ditto is a for-profit company. They are distributors of insurance and are paid a commission by the insurance companies for enrolling people like you & me into term insurance and health insurance plans. Payouts differ from insurer to insurer and product to product but as a thumbrule, the gross payout for term insurance is somewhere around 40% of first year's premium and 15-20% of health insurance premium (1st year and renewal). This commission pays for the distributors infrastructure, employees, marketing expenses, admin, compliance costs etc.

As Shrehith explained in the podcast, Ditto procures leads using inbound enquiries i.e. prospects have to book a call with Ditto advisors. Plus by offering above-par service, Ditto gets a lot of referrals also which is free marketing/leads. This is Ditto's operating model. In sharp contrast is Policybazaar which uses outbound telemarketing to pitch to prospects. At ET Money, the insurance process I had created was based around cross-selling to existing MF client with partial use of telecalling advisors. So every company follows a slightly different operating model which has a bearing on costs and profits.

I don't have specifics on exact revenue, costs, profits or margins but I hope the above helps you paint a picture

#### 8. @shankarnath — 7 likes — **reply to** `UgwT-SSQWqLVsPkZ1AR4AaABAg`

I like to draw a decision tree when in doubt.

So scenario 1 is I inform the insurer of a change in my health condition while I am alive --
1.a) Insurer acknowledges in the lines of "claim will not be affected" which is a big assurance. Or maybe doesn't acknowledge but still, I have sent the information on a timely basis
1.b) Insurer rejects or creates impediment (like saying I had pre-existing condition) which gives me an opportunity to represent myself to the ombudsperson or IRDAI or to a court

Scenario 2 is I keep mum about my health condition. I die over time and in in that case --
2.a) Insurer is a nice guy and processes claim ignoring my health condition plus following the 5-year moratorium clause as defined by the IRDAI
2.b) Insurer rejects claims and my beneficiary is hassled trying to disprove the insurer's contention

There are no right or wrong answers here, just probabilities (like most things in life)

#### 13. @shankarnath — 2 likes — **reply to** `Ugzq5SaBnVKhs7WBGkN4AaABAg`

Thank you! I'll try to do one for health insurance too. Insurers add/remove hospitals from time to time due to a number of reasons. The top ones are :

1. Expiry of agreement
2. Disagreement on fees -- the insurer and hospital agree on per treatment charges; conflict often erupt
3. Fraud by the hospital (over-billing, fictitious patients, forging reports etc.)
4. Hospital opting out due to delays in payment of cashless treatment charges (I think normal cycle is 30 days which means with cashless increasing the hospital has to have atleast 30 days of working capital which can run into a few crores)
5. Customer complaints

#### 77. @shankarnath — 0 likes — **reply to** `Ugz8oMm7Qc6YC7xdScR4AaABAg`

Hello, anyone can shutdown. Even with agent, I think lakhs of agents move into and out of this insurance middleman profession. That way established companies (and there are very few -- Ditto & Policybazaar being the prominent ones) are less likely to shut shop and move cities. The same applies to behaviour aswell. 

I look at the entire situation as a 1st, 2nd and 3rd recourse. So first step is to take deal with the insurer, if that doesn't work then the middleman comes in (Ditto or others) and finally there is the IRDAI/courts who will listen to our problems (if any). The point is -- one should give oneself the best opportunity of making a case incase a dispute arises

Ofcourse I really can't guess 20 years in the future if a company or an agent will be there or what their behaviour will be -- and neither can you. So its a game of probability, one should do what their rational mind says and where their comfort is.

#### 131. @KrishnaKumar-vx8lm — 0 likes — **reply to** `UgwRWUbMVqUtdXMQMlF4AaABAg.A4RDFlJqJRDA4SGtMZ9Vv7`

 @shankarnath  There's no need. Any health issue happening after the policy is bought need not be informed to the insurer. It's like the smoking example in the interview. Maybe a psychological relief for the customer. Could backfire if that info leads to some innocuous issues prior to policy date. 
Like , you send info about a hernia operation to life insurance company post the policy issue, they find (if a death claim arises) with that hospital number that you had viral fever 2 years ago ..which you did not disclose in proposal. Desperate companies could reject death claim based on that. Sec 45 doesn't cover anything within 3 years prior to date of issue ... even undisclosed viral fever is sufficient to reject a death claim though most companies would not. Coming from someone who has overseen processing of thousands of death claims 😊

#### 6. @achuthanachilles — 5 likes — **reply to** `root`

Hi Ditto, as a fellow insurance professional, I disagree with claims settlement ratio published as a valid criteria for selecting insurer. The ratio is skewed because of all the claims getting settled under corporate GTL. I would rather look at the solvency of the insurance company. I would look for an insurer who is not notorious for denying claim or finding a way to deny claim. Just like you mentioned in the video. Their claim settlement ratio could even be 99%.

Also I am more comfortable even if the ultimated holding company or promoter is based out of India. Foreign capital is tough to regulate in terms of crisis.

The biggest risk in purchasing life insurance is counterparty risk i.e solvency of the insurer.

#### 14. @Zero1byZerodha — 2 likes — **reply to** `UgzDv9Nkh650sqqeOW14AaABAg`

If you consider the time value of money alone, then it doesn't make sense to buy an insurance policy at the age of 25 as opposed to buying the policy at 35 assuming you buy the policy till 60 and you assume current prices. However there are a lot of assumptions baked in these calculations. They will change considerably if you assume you are buying a policy till 85 or 90. And what if we compare the same numbers assuming ages 25 and 30. As you can see there is a lot of variability and the financial equation could change based on these assumptions.

And there are intangibles that you can't quantify. You are less likely to develop complications when you are 25. This may have a big impact on your premiums. There's also the fact that you get coverage for 10 years. What happens if your dependents need the money during that time. Once you account for these intangibles, then it may make sense to buy a term insurance plan when you're young as opposed to deferring that purchase.

However, you should only make this decision based on your dependents. If you don't have dependents and you don't foresee dependents in the future, then there is no point in buying a term plan.

#### 17. @anantharaman7916 — 2 likes — **reply to** `root`

I'm assuming that your parents are not dependent on your income.

#### 44. @LabourLawAdvisor — 9 likes — **reply to** `UgwQbJ_ZPSnV0-12pNp4AaABAg.ACESO-M9idGACEXDFWa48r`

 @destinyissomething 

If you choose regular pay and invest 2300 monthly for 38 years @12% expected IRR you get 1.8 crore.

vs.

You choose limited pay start investing 4500 Monthly in 11th year for next 28 years @12% you get 1.1 crore

That's a difference of 70 lakh. For me it's pretty substantial. This is the opportunity cost. 

Compounding needs time, and in this case it's 10 years worth of compounding getting lost with limited pay.

#### 80. @Prithiv-IRDAI-InsuranceAdvisor — 3 likes — **reply to** `UgyfYYK_kL5TwX2G9sR4AaABAg.AC9RyVLi0tVACAFXZEhqIL`

 @sanketnegi1  Incorrect, this is just a myth.

the fact is: if one insurer rejects your claim, other insurers will follow the same by quoting the same from the rejected claim. it is not advisable to diversify when it comes to term insurance especially if you are breaking your sum insured across multiple plans/insurers

and breaking your sum insured into multiple plans will cost you more. 

it is mandatory to disclose about any policy that you already have to the new policy insurer. Hence they will get to know if your claim is paid or rejected

#### 132. @sumitagarwal5539 — 1 likes — **reply to** `root`

I think going for the regular pay has its own downsides. Yes, You can save premium and investing that premium in SIP to earn decent return,  I had the same thought process when i took my 1st Term Insurance in 2016. However, many people after paying few premiums through regular pay stop paying the same (for whatever reasons) after few years and because of that policy lapses. I find Limited pay of 10-12 years better, so that I dont need to worry about Insurance premiums after 10-12 years for the next 25-30 years (Hopefully!)..Hence, for my second term insurance, i opted for limited pay of 10 years in 2021. This is my thought process and I took a consious decision by opting for limited pay. So, the point is each individual need to understand his own psychology and his financial condition before choosing for the apt TERM Insurance. We need not paint all with the same brush

#### 419. @manish_k_27 — 0 likes — **reply to** `root`

Terms and conditions are scary Like the premature closure penalty in bank FD is said to be 1%, so I used to think that if I have opened an FD of Rs. 2 lakh for 3 years and suppose I am closing it after 2 years, then Suppose till now it has become 2 lakh 25 thousand, then 1% of that i.e. Rs 2250 will have to be paid as penalty.But later I came to know that the real rule is that suppose I made a 3 year FD and during this time I got 7% interest rate, but I closed this FD after 2 years So the penalty that I will be charged will be calculated as follows - the interest rate prevailing on a 2-year FD 2 years ago (say it was 6 percent). So I will get only 6-1 = 5% interest for 2 years.There is a lot of loss in this, so I feel that fd should be made of 399 days or 456 days or for a short term (less than 2 years). And the interest in these is also good.

#### 577. @Prithiv-IRDAI-InsuranceAdvisor — 0 likes — **reply to** `UgyPSHzPdut0zABLR1p4AaABAg`

Yes, you can claim the uncovered amount of the first insurer from second insurer. Having two policies makes sense it lot of angles like waiting period, non covered, better unique benefits. 

Note: To save cost on second insurance, you buy the second policy with a deductible which will reduce your premium significantly.

#### 718. @chaz-e — 0 likes — **reply to** `root`

1:30 you were referring to the conflicting idea of being customer and share holder of a company at the same time.

Revenue generation is not the most important metric, it is the profit/bottom line. Profit is dependent on product price and also the cost, so it up to the company to optimise cost and maximise profit. Thus maximize shareholder's profit too.

#### 752. @HeavenlyDemonicPotato — 0 likes — **reply to** `root`

Player A: with normal plan. Mutual fund value after 38 years = 2.54cr.

Player B: After taking 4.826/month for 10 years, and then 4,826/month sip in a MF for 28 years after ending payment of Term insurance. Value = 1.33cr

Player A has the better option. In every way. 
Because early or late death = extra money from MF, 
And no death will also give 2.54cr

#### 820. @mohitverma8897 — 0 likes — **reply to** `root`

Sir, the extra pay policy will end in 10 years, so how can you accumulate 2 crores with a 38-year SIP? Technically, if you invest in SIP for just 10 years, the total amount would be ₹560,090 if you assume a 12% ROI. However, you would need to continue the investment for the next 28 years. After 10 years, the ₹560,090 you saved can be invested as a lump sum at 12% ROI, which would grow to ₹13,377,114 only.

#### 50. @abhishekagrawal9950 — 6 likes — **reply to** `Ugy4HlHykTsW0jbI4kx4AaABAg`

Considering your age at 25 years, you can get a cover of 25 lakhs, a premium of ehich might come to around 6-8 thousands, just check claim settlement ratio of it, check how long is the waiting period for all types of deseases (eg:- covid and some deseases have a waiting period of 1 month, critical disease have 2 years, and pre-existing disease is 4 years), do check this, and also check how many cashless hospitals are there in your city where this policy will work..well i guess you can also check entire chronic disease list also..these all things i considered while buying my health insurance..maybe this will help u alot..thanx🙏🙏

#### 293. @funtaxguru2539 — 1 likes — **reply to** `root`

The offers with a huge lump sum looks very lucrative. But the risk of recovering the sum assured is very high in Term insurances. The claim settlement ratio given by the companies are those for which the death claim documents are submitted. But there are numerous instances where people could not arrange the documents sought by the insurance companies. By that way they do not honour the policy and also it is not counted for claim settlement ratio. 

So this policies are only for the nominees who have a better understanding of the law and regulations. But normally the policy holder buys the policy for the nominee who is dependent on him and may not be able to survive without him. These nominees can not get the death claim amount and the premium paid is just a waste of money. 

This is just my observation in some cases where i had to help the nominee to get her claim amount where she lost all the hopes to get the money because of the list of unnecessary documents sought by the insurance company.

#### 318. @rohitsoni9325 — 1 likes — **reply to** `UgwJujr92A5Z2J9qjOF4AaABAg`

1. You should have chosen disability rider which will insure you if you get a permanent disability but did not die as in this case also you probably won't be able to continue working. Also, you don't have to look for dependents at the current time but plan for dependents in future like your wife or children.
2. Generally its advised not to chose paying for lesser term than your cover benefit as over the time due to inflation premium will be negligible after 20-25 years. and yes its better to take term plan than whole life plans.
3. Simply, if you die your nominee will get the insurance amount. Here, die literally means DIE. Nominee wont get money if you are critically ill/ severely handicapped or any other situations). There are only two conditions:1. Your death should not be because of Suicide or drunk driving. 2. You should have declared everything truly. (Notify your lifestyle changes like started smoking/drinking.. they will increase some amount in your premium but you will be covered else when nominee tries to claim this they will find that you smoke/drink and didn't told them and they'll reject the claim. other than this, you are all good!

#### 749. @gouravacharya8571 — 0 likes — **reply to** `UgxXwv_yIXoFT6is87J4AaABAg`

There are options for increasing life cover as well as decreasing .after certain age your liabilities are decreased (after age of 70) so it's wise to take more coverage during youth stage till 70 and than reduce it by 25% or 50% . This way you will have less premium .
Also never keep regular pay paying term must always be till age of 60 because if in later stage you miss the payment even for one month your policy will lapse .hope it helps 
For more info. Pls contact me
